summary: Iron-deficiency anemia often shows low transferrin saturation but not necessarily elevated serum ferritin; latent cases exist globally especially due to poor nutrition. The exception here pertains specifically to reliance solely on serum ferritin for diagnosis at very early stages when other markers may also need consideration like soluble transferrin receptor or total iron-binding capacity alongside inflammatory marker assessments if suspected clinically based on context clues such as chronic infections etc.. Serum Ferritin alone cannot reliably diagnose all forms nor predict risk accurately enough independent from these complementary tests needed together ideally before definitive conclusions drawn
